picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: GOOGL hit an all-time high on a split-adjusted basis during today's session (intraday high $403.70), powered by Q1 2026 Google Cloud revenue growth of +63% YoY and a $102.35B quarterly revenue beat. The stock is a primary earnings revision driver in the S&P 500 — Druckenmiller increased his GOOGL stake by 276.7% in Q4 2025, and Ackman has also held a concentrated position. In a stagflation/higher-for-longer regime, GOOGL's advertising moat provides nominal GDP pass-through while Cloud is a structural secular story with expanding operating margins.
picks.catalyst: NVDA earnings May 20 as read-through for hyperscaler AI capex validation; Google I/O product cycle announcements + Waymo expansion (200 sq mi added today)
picks.catalystWindow: Next 2-4 weeks
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~$402, near 52-week high of $403.70 but up 158% from 52-week low of $156.16; forward P/E ~22x on NTM ~$18 EPS — reasonable for a business compounding cloud at 63% and advertising growing double-digits; consolidating above 50dma after the earnings gap-up
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 35% YoY for two consecutive quarters OR antitrust ruling forces Search distribution restructuring that materially impairs revenue; loss of Apple default search deal
picks.keyRisk: DOJ/EU antitrust ruling forcing structural remedies on Search; any slowdown in hyperscaler capex that compresses Cloud operating leverage

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's custom ASIC model — designing bespoke AI accelerators for hyperscalers — posted 74%+ AI semiconductor revenue growth YoY in fiscal Q4 2025 with management guiding above 100% in Q1 FY2026. With Q1 FY2026 EPS already at $2.05 vs. $1.88 estimated (9.04% beat), gross margins structurally above 75%, and Citigroup raising its price target to $500 today, AVGO is both the momentum and quality leader in custom silicon. ARK also added AVGO in January 2026, a rare confirmation across growth and institutional managers.
picks.catalyst: NVDA May 20 earnings as hyperscaler capex validation; Broadcom's own fiscal Q2 2026 earnings report (expected late May/early June) with AI revenue guidance update
picks.catalystWindow: Next 3-6 weeks
picks.entryNote: Trading around $419 (dipped today per broader market); 52-week range $221.60–$437.68; Citigroup just raised PT to $500 implying ~19% upside; GARP profile with strong FCF yield vs. peers
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a hyperscaler ASIC design win (especially Google TPU or Meta MTIA program) OR gross margin compression below 72% for two quarters
picks.keyRisk: Customer concentration — two or three hyperscalers represent the bulk of custom silicon revenue; losing one contract is an outsized earnings event

picks.name: Exxon Mobil Corporation
picks.rationale: With Brent/WTI above $100–$104/barrel on Strait of Hormuz disruptions and Trump reportedly weighing renewed combat operations in Iran, XOM is the highest-quality integrated energy beneficiary in the universe. Energy is confirmed Tier 1 in the sector rotation framework, Berkshire added CVX in Q4 2025 as an inflation/commodity hedge, and Druckenmiller's EWZ bet signals a broader commodity/real-asset conviction. XOM's integrated model (upstream + refining + chemicals) monetizes high oil price across the entire chain and generates massive FCF for buybacks at $100+ oil.
picks.catalyst: Iran conflict escalation risk and SPR release exhaustion keeping oil elevated; Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with upstream volume guidance
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing/continuous; Q2 guidance in July–August
picks.entryNote: Oil at $104/bbl supports $10–12/share earnings power; XOM trading at ~9–10x forward earnings — historically cheap for an integrated major vs. 5-year median of ~13x; sector momentum confirmed leader per rotation analysis
picks.exitTrigger: Brent crude sustained below $75/barrel for 30+ days (demand destruction recession) OR Strait of Hormuz reopens and Iran ceasefire removes risk premium
picks.keyRisk: Geopolitical resolution — a ceasefire or Hormuz reopening would remove the risk premium rapidly; demand destruction from global stagflation also a meaningful downside scenario

picks.name: RTX Corporation (Raytheon Technologies)
picks.rationale: RTX is the purest expression of the Iran war defense premium within the concentrated book — Raytheon's missile and munitions divisions (Patriot, AIM-120 AMRAAM, StormBreaker) are in sustained high-demand as U.S. and allied munitions stockpiles are actively replenished. The Iran conflict has already cost U.S. taxpayers $29B+ with potential to reach $1T per Harvard estimates, implying a multi-year defense spending tailwind. RTX offers both the geopolitical optionality premium of NOC/LMT with superior commercial aerospace revenue from Pratt & Whitney engines providing a cyclical floor.
picks.catalyst: Pentagon supplemental defense appropriations (likely in Q2–Q3 2026 Congressional session) and allied NATO procurement decisions driven by Iran conflict duration
picks.catalystWindow: June–September 2026
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~18–19x forward P/E with a ~2.2% dividend yield; defense sector consensus overweight; strong earnings revision trajectory driven by record backlog; Pratt & Whitney GTF engine demand provides commercial aerospace floor
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire declared and defense supplemental appropriations fail to materialize; Pratt & Whitney GTF powder metal disk issue causes grounding of additional aircraft fleets beyond original estimates
picks.keyRisk: Iran peace resolution removing the geopolitical risk premium; ongoing GTF engine recall/inspection liability that could dwarf current reserves

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: LLY remains the single-best large-cap secular growth story outside of AI infrastructure — GLP-1 drugs (Mounjaro/Zepbound) represent a decade-long revenue supercycle. LLY is the largest holding in XLV at ~15% of the ETF and has been a consistent top-5 position in this book since May 5. The GLP-1 market is supply-constrained, and LLY's manufacturing capacity expansion (multiple new sites) will unlock revenue as production scales. In a stagflationary environment, LLY's inelastic demand and pricing power insulate it from consumer cyclicality headwinds.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readout for orforglipron (oral GLP-1 pill) — a potential game-changer for the obesity market; Q2 2026 earnings with Zepbound volume/ASP guidance update
picks.catalystWindow: Q3 2026 (orforglipron); Q2 earnings July 2026
picks.entryNote: Pulled back ~15% from highs in 2025 on GLP-1 competitive noise; valuation around 35–38x forward earnings — elevated but justified by 30–40% EPS growth runway over 3–5 years; manufacturing scale-up is the key overhang resolving in 2026
picks.exitTrigger: Orforglipron Phase 3 failure OR unexpected safety signal on Mounjaro/Zepbound that triggers FDA safety review; sustained market share loss to Novo Nordisk Rybelsus/Wegovy combination
picks.keyRisk: Competitive pressure from NVO's next-gen CagriSema (cagrilintide + semaglutide) Phase 3 readout; political drug pricing risk from IRA most-favored-nation provisions

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: GS is the premier quality/momentum financial in the universe — 4/4 EPS beats, FY2026 estimates revised upward on IB recovery and FICC outperformance tied to the Iran conflict-driven commodity volatility. The financials sector blended Q1 2026 revenue growth rate improved to 10.9%, and the steepening 2s10s yield curve (+51 bps as of May 1) directly benefits Goldman's NIM-adjacent trading revenues. Druckenmiller established a $300M+ XLF position in Q4 2025 as his macro bet on yield curve steepening — GS is the single-stock expression of that thesis.
picks.catalyst: Kevin Warsh assumes Fed Chair May 15 — his hawkish posture may re-steepen the curve further, boosting FICC revenues; Q2 2026 IB fee pipeline update in July earnings
picks.catalystWindow: May 15 (Fed transition); July 2026 Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading at ~12–14x forward earnings, well below 10-year historical median of ~11x on tangible book; strong YTD relative outperformance vs. SPY (+18% relative per prior data); dividend + buyback yield ~3.5%
picks.exitTrigger: 2s10s spread inverts again below 0 bps OR IB deal pipeline dries up on growth scare; regulatory capital surcharge increase under Basel III endgame that compresses ROE below 12%
picks.keyRisk: Macro growth scare triggering M&A freeze — IB revenues are highly cyclical and could compress rapidly if CEO confidence falls; also exposed to any AI capital market bubble correction

picks.name: Amazon.com Inc.
picks.rationale: AWS revenue accelerated to +28% growth in Q1 2026 — its fastest pace in 15 quarters — making AMZN the cloud acceleration story with the most fundamental momentum in the book. Advertising (the highest-margin segment alongside AWS) is also growing double-digits, expanding the FCF generation flywheel. While Berkshire trimmed AMZN aggressively, that reflects a different mandate — for a growth book, AMZN's AWS re-acceleration + advertising mix improvement creates a multi-year FCF margin expansion thesis that the market has not yet fully priced.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 AWS guidance update (expected late July); Trump-Xi summit May 14–15 trade outcome could remove tariff headwinds on retail/logistics margins
picks.catalystWindow: May 14–15 (trade summit); July 2026 Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading around $265 per Google Finance data; YTD down ~7% per prior brief — creating a valuation reset opportunity vs. the fundamental AWS acceleration thesis; forward P/E ~35–37x with FCF yield improving as capex converts to operating leverage
picks.exitTrigger: AWS growth decelerates below 20% YoY for two consecutive quarters; retail gross margin compression sustained below 28% due to tariff pass-through costs
picks.keyRisk: Tariff escalation significantly compressing retail margins while AWS faces margin pressure from buildout; antitrust action on AWS or advertising monopoly allegations

picks.name: S&P Global Inc.
picks.rationale: SPGI is the highest-quality 'picks and shovels' play on the current macro regime — its Ratings division benefits from elevated debt issuance volumes as companies refinance in a higher-rate environment, and its Market Intelligence/Indices divisions are secular growth businesses with 90%+ recurring revenue and near-zero variable costs. In an environment with HY OAS at 335–365 bps and IG at 90–100 bps, credit market activity remains robust, directly monetizing SPGI's rating franchise. ROIC consistently above 30% and FY2026 estimates in sustained upward revision.
picks.catalyst: Sustained debt issuance wave as corporates refinance ahead of potential rate hike cycle; Platts commodity data/analytics division growing rapidly on energy market volatility driven by Iran conflict
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing 2026; Q2 earnings July 2026
picks.entryNote: Trades at approximately 28–30x forward earnings — premium justified by subscription-revenue mix (70%+), 30%+ ROIC, and secular data analytics growth; has been a consistent top-10 holding in this book since May 8
picks.exitTrigger: Debt capital markets activity collapses on recession; regulatory mandate requiring ratings competition reduces SPGI's pricing power; Merger-era synergies with IHS Markit fully realized with no new growth vectors
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory action on ratings duopoly; any significant debt market freeze (credit spread widening above 600 bps HY) that halts new issuance and dries up ratings revenue

picks.name: Vistra Corp.
picks.rationale: VST is a unique utilities play that behaves like a growth stock — it is the largest competitive power generator in the U.S., with nuclear (Comanche Peak) and natural gas assets that directly benefit from data center electricity demand. Unlike regulated utilities (rate-base constraints), VST operates merchant power with market-based pricing, meaning it is an inflation pass-through in real time. With PPI energy up 17.9% YoY and oil above $104, VST's power pricing environment is structurally supportive, and the company's Q1 2026 results showed nuclear margins expanding as CEG fell 7.8% today on execution concerns.
picks.catalyst: Data center power purchase agreement (PPA) announcements with hyperscalers (Microsoft, Google, Amazon have active procurement programs); Q2 2026 power price curve resets
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; any new data center PPA announcement is an immediate re-rating event
picks.entryNote: Has been in this book since May 6; trades at ~15–17x forward earnings vs. regulated utility peers at 18–19x — cheaper because of perceived merchant risk, which is actually a tailwind in an inflationary power-price environment; offers ~2% dividend + buyback yield
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained power price decline below $35/MWh in ERCOT (Texas grid) OR natural gas price collapse that eliminates the merchant premium; nuclear regulatory issue at Comanche Peak
picks.keyRisk: CEG fell 7.8% today on execution issues — VST faces similar nuclear operational risk; ERCOT power prices are volatile and a warm winter/mild summer could sharply compress merchant margins

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: META's Q1 2026 EPS of $10.44 vs. $6.67 estimated represented one of the largest positive surprises in the S&P 500 this quarter (~57% beat), with AI-driven ad tools (Advantage+) visibly compounding gross margins. Communication Services earnings growth surged to 48.8% in Q1, with META the primary driver. While the stock has re-rated post-earnings and the easy money has been made, the AI monetization flywheel — Llama models driving advertiser efficiency, Reality Labs cost discipline improving, and WhatsApp monetization in early innings — supports sustained earnings revision momentum through 2026.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 advertising revenue guidance (late July) — particularly any acceleration in AI-driven ARPU from Advantage+ rollout; Llama ecosystem open-source developer traction creating infrastructure defensibility
picks.catalystWindow: July 2026 Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Reduced to medium-conviction and smaller weight vs. GOOGL given the 57% EPS beat creates a tough comp for Q2; forward P/E ~24–26x — elevated vs. historical median but defensible given 40%+ earnings growth; been in the book since May 8 at high conviction, step-down in weight reflects post-beat comp risk
picks.exitTrigger: Q2 2026 ad revenue guidance misses Street estimates by more than 3%; EU DSA enforcement action materially restricts targeted advertising; Reality Labs operating losses re-accelerate above -$6B/quarter
picks.keyRisk: Regulatory overhang in Europe (DSA/DMA) limiting AI-targeted ad capabilities; advertiser pullback in a consumer slowdown; Q2 comp vs. the extraordinary Q1 beat creates elevated miss risk

picks.name: SPDR Gold Shares ETF
picks.rationale: Gold is the canonical stagflation hedge and is trading near $4,600/oz — near record levels — with central bank buying from China, India, and Poland providing a structural demand floor. With PPI up 6% YoY, CPI at 3.8%, rate hike odds at 36%, and Kevin Warsh assuming the Fed chair role on May 15, the institutional demand for real assets as a store of value and a hedge against fiscal dominance risk is at a multi-year high. GLD has near-zero equity beta in genuine risk-off events and provides the most direct hedge against the tail risk of Fed independence erosion.
picks.catalyst: Kevin Warsh Fed chair assumption May 15 — any hawkish signal that raises real rate uncertainty; April PCE release (late May) that confirms the CPI/PPI regime
picks.catalystWindow: May 15; ongoing structural bid
picks.entryNote: Spot gold near $4,600 — pulled back slightly from recent highs as dollar strengthened on CPI/PPI data; near-term tactical re-entry as dollar strength is a transient effect of inflation data rather than a structural trend given USD already weak YTD at DXY ~100–101
picks.exitTrigger: Iran ceasefire + rapid CPI normalization toward 2% target over 2 quarters, which would reduce the stagflation/fiscal-dominance risk premium substantially
picks.keyRisk: A rapid Iran ceasefire and energy price normalization removing the geopolitical premium simultaneously with a Fed credibility restoration event (hot dot plot + Warsh hawkishness)

picks.name: Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF
picks.rationale: SCHD provides systematic exposure to high-quality dividend-paying U.S. companies screened for FCF, dividend growth, and yield — blended exposure to Financials, Healthcare, Industrials, and Staples at a meaningfully lower P/E than the cap-weighted S&P 500. In a 'higher for longer' rate environment where equity risk premiums are compressed and the market is pricing at ~21x forward earnings, SCHD's ~3.5–4% dividend yield provides a competitive income floor while the factor tilt (quality + low volatility) historically outperforms in flat-to-down equity environments.
picks.catalyst: Any equity market correction driven by rate hike pricing would cause a rotation into quality/dividend factor — SCHD's factor exposure is exactly what institutional allocators reach for in that scenario
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; tactical inflow acceleration if rate hike probabilities rise above 50%
picks.entryNote: Consistent position in this book since May 6; factor P/E ~16–18x vs. SPY at ~21x — meaningful valuation discount with similar earnings quality; ~3.5–4% dividend yield provides income while holding quality compounders
picks.exitTrigger: Rate cuts materialize and risk appetite surges, causing growth-factor outperformance that makes SCHD's defensive tilt a drag; reduce if defensiveScore drops to 2 or below
picks.keyRisk: Factor crowding — quality/dividend stocks have seen significant inflows; if growth re-accelerates sharply, SCHD's factor tilt underperforms on the upside

picks.name: Berkshire Hathaway Inc. Class B
picks.rationale: BRK.B is the closest thing to a 'quality compounder with embedded optionality' in the universe — $380B in cash at record highs under new CEO Greg Abel, with the option to deploy aggressively in a market dislocation. Its sprawling insurance, energy (OxyChem acquired Jan 2026), utilities, and consumer franchise generates durable FCF independent of AI narratives. As a de-facto safe harbor with low beta and no single-product concentration risk, it provides portfolio ballast while simultaneously holding a call option on macro deterioration (Abel can deploy capital at distressed prices).
picks.catalyst: Q1 2026 13F filing due May 15 — market will scrutinize whether Abel deployed any of the $380B cash hoard; any new major acquisition announcement would be an immediate re-rating catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: May 15 (13F filing); any acquisition announcement
picks.entryNote: Trades at ~1.4–1.6x book value — historically Buffett repurchases aggressively below 1.5x, providing a valuation floor; ~21x earnings for the quality, diversification, and $380B optionality is highly reasonable vs. S&P 500 at 21x with concentrated AI risk
picks.exitTrigger: Greg Abel makes a large, fundamentally questionable acquisition (similar to the Kraft Heinz mistake) that destroys capital; cash hoard deployed at elevated valuations with poor return prospects
picks.keyRisk: Opportunity cost risk — $380B in cash earns ~4% but misses equity upside if markets continue to melt up; Abel's capital allocation decisions are unproven vs. Buffett's track record

picks.name: iShares 7-10 Year Treasury ETF
picks.rationale: IEF provides intermediate Treasury duration (~7–8 years) with a ~4.4–4.5% yield — a competitive risk-adjusted return given the 10-year is at 4.47–4.48% (its highest level in 2026) following the CPI+PPI double shock this week. In a genuine growth scare or recession scenario (the second tail risk in the risk assessment framework), intermediate Treasuries rally sharply as rate cuts are priced back in, making IEF the most asymmetric defensive instrument when the macro narrative pivots from 'stagflation' to 'growth scare.' Less duration risk than TLT while capturing meaningful term premium.
picks.catalyst: Any softening in May non-farm payrolls (due early June) or retail sales data that suggests demand destruction from energy prices is hitting the consumer — would reprice rate cut odds and cause IEF to rally meaningfully
picks.catalystWindow: June 2026 (May NFP); ongoing carry at 4.4%
picks.entryNote: 10-year yield at 4.47–4.48% today, highest of 2026 — tactically attractive entry as yields approach cycle highs; duration ~7.3 years means a 50 bps yield decline produces ~3.65% capital gain on top of the carry; sized small (4%) as a growth-scare hedge only
picks.exitTrigger: 10-year Treasury yield breaks and holds above 5.0% on sustained basis (fiscal dominance scenario) OR inflation re-accelerates beyond 4.5% headline making duration inherently dangerous; in that case, reduce to zero
picks.keyRisk: Bear steepener continues — if the 30-year and 10-year sell off further on term premium expansion and fiscal concerns, IEF loses value despite holding shorter duration than TLT

picks.name: The Coca-Cola Company
picks.rationale: KO is the quintessential stagflation-resilient compounder — 60+ years of consecutive dividend increases, near-100% global distribution moat, and proven pricing power through the 2021–2024 inflationary cycle. Consumer staples led gains on May 13 per Edward Jones sector data, validating the defensive rotation thesis. In a regime where real wages fell -0.5% MoM and -0.3% annually, consumers still buy Coca-Cola products, making KO's inelastic demand profile a genuine storm shelter. The ~3.1% dividend yield provides income superior to the 10-year real yield in an inflationary environment.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (July) with pricing power confirmation; any consumer spending deterioration data accelerating defensive rotation into staples sector
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing; July 2026 Q2 earnings
picks.entryNote: Trading near fair value at ~24x forward earnings — premium to the market average justified by business model predictability and dividend aristocrat status; near-zero correlation to AI/tech narrative risk; been a periodic position in this book since May 7
picks.exitTrigger: Sustained organic volume decline for two consecutive quarters suggesting brand/consumer spending fatigue beyond pricing; generic/private label market share gains accelerating in a deep recession scenario
picks.keyRisk: Currency headwinds — 60%+ of revenue is international and a strengthening USD (already gaining on CPI data today) compresses reported earnings; also faces GLP-1 secular headwind if obesity drug adoption reduces soda consumption at scale

summary: The regime as of May 13, 2026 is confirmed late-cycle stagflation — CPI at 3.8% YoY and PPI at +6.0% YoY (both hotter than consensus), with futures now pricing just 3% odds of a rate cut in 2026 and 36% odds of a rate hike, while the S&P 500 and Nasdaq paradoxically closed at record highs driven by AI/tech momentum ignoring the inflation data. The 10-name growth book expresses this regime through four interlocking themes: AI infrastructure cash-flow leaders (GOOGL, AVGO, AMZN, META) that generate real nominal earnings in an inflationary environment; geopolitical/commodity beneficiaries (XOM, RTX) that directly monetize the Iran conflict risk premium with WTI above $104; financial sector beneficiaries of the steepening yield curve and IB recovery (GS, SPGI); and selective secular growth with pricing power insulation (LLY, VST). All seven core positions are direct carry-overs from the May 12–13 book — thesis continuity is intact with no forced rotation warranted. The only changes are META stepping down from high to medium conviction (tough Q2 comp after the 57% EPS beat) and the growth book maintaining the same fundamental structure. The 5-name defensive sleeve (GLD, SCHD, BRK.B, IEF, KO) is carried over intact from May 12, providing stagflation hedging (GLD), quality-income ballast (SCHD, KO), optionality on dislocation (BRK.B with $380B cash), and a growth-scare duration hedge (IEF at 4.47% yield). The dominant risk being underwritten is that the AI melt-up continues to defy the macro gravity of 6% PPI and 36% rate hike odds — a 'rational bubble' scenario where earnings momentum temporarily overrides valuation concerns — which could compress the defensives underperform, a risk we accept given the 1–5 year horizon demands protection against the eventual re-rating when the inflation/rate reality reasserts.
diversificationNote: The 10-name growth book spans 5 distinct GICS sectors: Communication Services (GOOGL, META — two names sharing the ad/AI monetization theme but with different moats: Search vs. Social), Information Technology (AVGO — custom silicon/ASIC), Energy (XOM — integrated oil/Iran hedge), Industrials (RTX — defense/aerospace), Health Care (LLY — GLP-1 secular), Financials (GS, SPGI — NIM/IB and data/ratings), Utilities (VST — merchant power/data center), and Consumer Discretionary (AMZN — cloud+ad, treated as IT-adjacent). The two Financials names (GS and SPGI) are accepted as sector concentration but are entirely different business models — capital markets revenues vs. recurring subscription/ratings revenue — with near-zero earnings correlation. The defensive sleeve spans four distinct exposures: gold commodity (GLD), broad dividend/quality income equity (SCHD), diversified conglomerate with embedded cash optionality (BRK.B as consumer-staples/insurance/energy proxy), intermediate Treasury duration (IEF), and consumer staples single-name (KO) — explicitly no double-up on any single exposure per construction rules.

picks.name: Alphabet Inc.
picks.rationale: Alphabet delivered Q1 2026 EPS of $5.11 — a +93.6% beat vs. consensus — on 21.8% revenue growth, with Google Cloud surging 63% to $20B and Cloud backlog nearly doubling to $462B. The AI monetization flywheel (Search AI Overviews, Gemini embedded in Workspace, Cloud AI workloads) is compounding into the world's deepest distribution network while forward estimates have been aggressively revised upward. Ackman, Viking Global, Tiger Global, and Coatue all hold GOOGL as a core position, providing heavyweight institutional validation.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with continued Cloud re-acceleration and AI Overviews monetization data; April CPI print May 12 as a benign macro relief catalyst
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks (May 12 CPI) + next 2 quarters (Q2 earnings)
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$337, up ~30% over 6 months vs. SPY; forward P/E of approximately 20x for a business growing cloud at 63% is deeply undemanding relative to peers — not technically extended given the Q1 earnings reset higher
picks.exitTrigger: Google Cloud growth decelerates below 30% YoY for two consecutive quarters, or regulatory breakup order targeting Search materially advances in courts
picks.keyRisk: AI-driven search disruption (Perplexity, OpenAI SearchGPT) eroding query volume and CPCs faster than Cloud offsets

picks.name: Broadcom Inc.
picks.rationale: Broadcom's Q1 FY2026 beat of +9% vs. estimates on 29% revenue growth (semiconductor segment +52%) reflects structurally durable demand for custom AI ASICs from Google, Meta, and ByteDance — the ASIC TAM is the most defensible moat in semiconductors. VMware's infrastructure software suite layered onto the chip business produces high-margin recurring revenue that insulates earnings in a cyclical downturn. 48 of 50 analysts rate it a Buy. Today's dip on OpenAI chip-deal financing noise ($418 vs. 52-week high $437) is a tactical entry opportunity rather than a thesis break, as the OpenAI relationship is a marginal contributor — the hyperscaler ASIC engine (Google TPU, Meta MTIA) is the core.
picks.catalyst: Q2 FY2026 earnings (est. June 5, 2026) — next formal update on AI ASIC backlog and VMware integration margins
picks.catalystWindow: Next 4 weeks for post-noise stabilization; June 5 earnings event
picks.entryNote: Trading ~$418 on May 8, pulled ~4% intraday on OpenAI deal financing headlines — 52-week range $203–$437; forward P/E ~43x on 25%+ growth is premium but justified given VMware recurring revenue contribution; entry here is meaningfully below ATH
picks.exitTrigger: Loss of a second major hyperscaler ASIC design win (beyond OpenAI noise) or VMware customer churn accelerating above 10% annually
picks.keyRisk: OpenAI custom chip financing difficulties signal a broader risk that non-hyperscaler ASIC customers lack balance sheet depth to fund $10B+ chip programs, reducing future ASIC TAM

picks.name: Meta Platforms Inc.
picks.rationale: Meta's Q1 2026 revenue grew 33% YoY with record operating margins as AI-driven ad ranking tools (Andromeda, Advantage+) continue extracting higher CPMs from the same inventory base — a monetization flywheel requiring zero incremental distribution cost. Management's capex raise post-Q1 signals confidence in monetization ROI rather than speculative spending. Bill Ackman initiated META as an 11.4% portfolio position in Q4 2025 — his largest new buy in years — citing the AI-ad flywheel and dominant platform moat.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (late July) with AI ad monetization metrics and Reality Labs cost discipline; ongoing AI advertising tool rollout to SMBs via Meta Advantage+
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (AI ad product cycle) + Q2 earnings late July
picks.entryNote: Gross margins above 80%, FCF generation elite; forward P/E approximately 25x for 30%+ revenue growth implies a PEG ratio below 1.0 — manageable multiple for the quality of compounding; technically consolidating above prior resistance
picks.exitTrigger: Two consecutive quarters of ad revenue growth decelerating below 15%, or regulatory break-up of WhatsApp/Instagram from Facebook core
picks.keyRisk: Generative AI-native social platforms (character.ai, TikTok successors) capture Gen-Z engagement at scale, structurally reducing Meta's time-on-app metrics

picks.name: Goldman Sachs Group Inc.
picks.rationale: Goldman Sachs is the highest-beta expression of the macro regime's most favorable impulses: a steepening yield curve (+50bp 10Y/2Y spread), HY OAS near historically tight 277bp supporting active M&A and IPO deal pipelines, and capital markets revenue surging as the Iran War creates advisory demand in energy, defense, and geopolitical M&A. The Financials sector (XLF) received Druckenmiller's single largest buy in Q4 2025, and Lone Pine Capital holds Financials as its largest sector allocation. Record blended S&P margins at 13.4% also support GS's asset/wealth management fee revenue.
picks.catalyst: Q2 2026 earnings (mid-July) with investment banking revenue disclosures — IPO/M&A advisory backlog is the key metric given elevated deal activity
picks.catalystWindow: Ongoing (deal market open) + Q2 earnings mid-July
picks.entryNote: Financials sector trades at a discount to the index per the macro research; GS at roughly 13x forward earnings vs. historical 11-14x range is fair-to-attractive for a capital markets cycle leader; has been a consistent top-5 hold since May 4
picks.exitTrigger: HY OAS widens above 450bp (stress signal), 10Y/2Y yield curve inverts again, or Q2 investment banking revenue declines more than 20% QoQ
picks.keyRisk: Iran War escalation triggers a risk-off credit event that freezes deal markets and widens spreads sharply — GS is the most direct casualty in that scenario

picks.name: Eli Lilly and Company
picks.rationale: Eli Lilly's tirzepatide franchise (Mounjaro/Zepbound) is structurally independent of the AI capex cycle and Iran War inflation shock — demand for GLP-1 weight-loss and diabetes therapy vastly exceeds supply through at least 2027. The regulatory moat (clinical data superiority, manufacturing scale barriers, early-2030s patent protection) makes this a multi-decade compounder. Healthcare sector forward P/E of ~16x vs. S&P at 20.9x offers valuation support, and LLY has de-rated from 2024 peak levels providing a better entry — confirmed by the risk officer's designation as a top defensive opportunity.
picks.catalyst: Phase 3 readout for orforglipron (oral GLP-1) expected H2 2026 — positive data would expand the total addressable market dramatically by removing injection barrier

phaseData.macro:
Here is your full macro regime briefing as of **Friday, May 8, 2026**:

---

# 🏦 U.S. EQUITY MACRO REGIME BRIEFING — MAY 8, 2026

---

## 📊 THE DATA DASHBOARD

### Fed Policy

At its April 29 meeting, the FOMC left the federal funds target range at **3.50%–3.75%**.
 
The Fed acknowledged that Middle East developments have increased uncertainty around the economic outlook and said officials are watching risks to both inflation and employment.
 
The decision kept the Fed in a wait-and-see posture; nearly all voting members supported holding rates steady, while one favored a 0.25% rate cut, and three others disagreed with the statement's easing bias rather than the rate decision itself — showing a divide over how strongly the Fed should signal potential future cuts.
 
The Fed's median projection (March dot plot) still points to **one quarter-point cut in 2026**.
 
The March 2026 dot plot projected a 3.00%–3.25% Fed funds rate by year-end 2027.
 
Core PCE inflation stood at **3.0%** in February 2026, well above the 2% target.
 With the EFFR at 3.625% (midpoint) and the neutral rate (r*) widely estimated at 2.5–3.0%, the **real policy rate is mildly restrictive** — but the geopolitical energy shock is complicating the calculus.

---

### Yield Curve

Long-end yields drifted lower at month-end, with the **10-year easing to 4.39%** and the **30-year settling at 4.97%**.
 The 2-year is implied near ~3.90% given the prevailing spread data. 
The 10-year/2-year spread is **+0.50pp** and the 10-year/3-month spread is **+0.75pp**.
 
Breakeven inflation expectations ticked higher: the 5-year breakeven rose to **2.72%** and the 10-year breakeven climbed to **2.50%**, suggesting markets are pricing in a small upward bias on inflation expectations as the next FOMC meeting approaches.
 The curve is positively sloped but **flat** — not a recessionary signal, but term premium direction is being pushed higher by energy-driven inflation worries on the long end.

---

### Inflation

In March, CPI for all urban consumers rose **0.9% MoM** and **+3.3% YoY**, while the core CPI (all items less food and energy) increased **0.2% MoM** and **+2.6% YoY**.
 
Over the year through March 2026, the headline PCE price index was **3.5%**, 1.1 percentage points above the 2.4% pace over the year through March 2025, with fluctuations in energy prices a large contributor to the pickup.
 
CPI inflation for energy goods and services averaged **3.2% per month in Q1 2026**, and on a 12-month basis, energy price inflation was **+12.5%** through March 2026, contrasting with a 3.3% decline a year earlier.
 
The ISM Services Prices Paid index held at **70.7** in April 2026 — the highest two-month average since October 2022, with all 18 industries tracked by the ISM reporting higher input costs.
 The inflation trajectory is clearly **re-accelerating**, driven by the energy/geopolitical shock; core is stickier than the Fed would like but not yet runaway.

---

### Growth

As of **May 8**, the Atlanta Fed GDPNow model estimates real GDP growth (SAAR) for Q2 2026 at **+3.7%**, with the nowcast of real gross private domestic investment growth rising to 9.2%, partly offset by a slight decline in personal consumption expenditures growth to 2.6%.
 
The first BEA estimate of Q1 real GDP growth came in at **+2.0%**, 0.8pp above GDPNow's final Q1 nowcast.
 
April nonfarm payrolls came in at **+115K** (well above consensus of ~62K), with gains concentrated in health care (+37K), transportation and warehousing (+30K), and retail trade (+22K).
 
The ISM Manufacturing PMI held at **52.7** in April 2026, matching its highest level since August 2022.
 
The ISM Services PMI inched down to **53.6** in April from 54 in March, loosely aligned with expectations but remaining firmly above prior-year averages.
 
The services prices gauge held at 70.7, with companies citing higher fuel, diesel, copper, and freight costs due to the war, plus aluminum and lumber costs from tariffs.


---

### Credit & Liquidity

ICE BofA US HY OAS was **~277 bps** (2.77%) in May 2026.
 This is extraordinarily tight by historical standards (long-run average ~500 bps), signaling risk-on complacency — or genuine credit quality at cycle highs. 
The 2026 credit market outlook calls for a rangebound rate environment and a modest increase in spreads within corporate credit markets; markets expect the Fed to lower the funds rate toward 3%, keeping the 10-year Treasury yield between 3.75%–4.25%.
 
Worker wages continue to outpace inflation, despite elevated price levels associated with the Iranian conflict.
 The DXY is showing modest weakness reflecting the Fed's on-hold posture and safe-haven demand for energy-importing currencies.

---

### Earnings Backdrop

With 63% of S&P 500 companies having reported Q1 2026, **84%** have beaten EPS estimates — above the 5-year average of 78% and the 10-year average of 76%.
 
In aggregate, companies are reporting earnings **20.7% above estimates**, which would mark the highest surprise percentage since Q1 2021 (22.2%).
 
The blended net profit margin for Q1 2026 stood at **13.4%** — a new record since FactSet began tracking the metric in 2009, surpassing the prior record of 13.2% set in Q4 2025.
 
The forward 12-month P/E ratio is **20.9×**, above the 5-year average (19.9×) and above the 10-year average (18.9×).
 
For CY 2026, analysts are predicting year-over-year earnings growth of **+21.3%**.
 
The Q2 bottom-up EPS estimate increased 2.1% (to **$80.47 from $78.84**) from March 31 to April 30 — an unusual positive revision in the first month of a quarter.
 
The S&P 500 is trading at approximately **$7,327** as of May 8, 2026.


---

### Geopolitical / Event Tape

According to ISM's April survey commentary, sentiment remained mixed in the second month of the Iran War, with 69% of comments negative, and the war mentioned in 47% of responses alongside tariffs in 18%.
 
Geopolitical uncertainty remains an upside risk to the medium-term inflation outlook.
 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
 — creating a leadership transition wildcard for H2 2026 monetary policy.

---

## 📋 THE THREE PARAGRAPHS

---

### 1. 🔴 Regime Call: **Late-Cycle / Stagflationary Overhang**

This is a **late-cycle regime with an embedded stagflationary shock** — not yet contraction, but structurally more fragile than mid-2025. 
The FOMC left rates at 3.50%–3.75%
, sitting above consensus neutral (~2.75–3.00%), meaning real policy rates are mildly restrictive even as nominal growth remains elevated. 
Headline PCE has re-accelerated to 3.5% YoY
 while 
the labor market is gradually cooling but remaining broadly resilient.
 The key tension: 
the ISM Services Prices Paid index held at 70.7 in April
 — the classic late-cycle stagflationary fingerprint of cost-push inflation running ahead of output deceleration. The Fed is trapped: it cannot ease without stoking inflation, and 
federal government employment continued to decline
 while manufacturing employment is contracting, narrowing the Fed's ability to declare mission accomplished. We are in a **late-cycle expansion with an energy-shock overlay** — bullish earnings momentum has extended the runway, but the clock is ticking.

---

### 2. ✅ What's Working / ❌ What's Breaking

**Working:**
- 
**Information Technology, Communication Services, Financials, and Real Estate** are all reporting year-over-year revenue growth
, and 
Alphabet, Amazon, and Meta were the three largest contributors to index earnings growth, dominating Q1 upside.
 AI capex beneficiaries (semis, cloud infrastructure, power) remain the dominant alpha sources.
- 
Record blended net profit margins at 13.4%
 favor **quality compounders** — companies with pricing power and lean cost structures, particularly in Tech and Industrials.
- **Energy** sector: 
12-month energy price inflation at +12.5%
 is a direct tailwind for integrated oils, LNG exporters, and defense-adjacent energy infrastructure.
- 
HY OAS near 277 bps
 signals benign credit conditions — **IG credit** and **leveraged finance** markets remain open for business.

**Breaking:**
- 
Manufacturing employment is declining at the sharpest pace in four months (ISM Employment sub-index at 46.4)
, and 
services employment contracted for a second consecutive month.

- 
New orders in Services sank 7.1 points to 53.5 — the sharpest decrease in three years — as consumer order volumes were dented by the surge in prices since the start of the war.

- 
Valuations now embed an expectation that the earnings trajectory continues, and the market's selective reaction to Big Tech capital spending plans suggests investors are no longer giving open-ended credit for AI investment without evidence of return.

- **Consumer discretionary** and **small-cap / high-beta cyclicals**: with 
5-year breakeven inflation at 2.72%
 and the Fed on hold, rate-sensitive sectors face continued headwinds. Rate cuts look increasingly H2 2026 at best.

---

### 3. 👁️ What to Watch: The 3 Highest-Impact Catalysts

1. **April CPI (May 12, 8:30am ET) — #1 Priority.** 
The Consumer Price Index for April 2026 is scheduled to be released on May 12.
 The market must resolve whether the energy shock's 0.9% MoM March print was a one-off or is feeding into core. If April core CPI surprises above ~0.3% MoM, the one remaining Fed cut in the dot plot gets repriced away entirely, long-end yields spike, and the 20.9× forward P/E becomes untenable. A benign print (~0.2% core) reopens the easing narrative and would be a relief catalyst.

2. **Iran War / Middle East Escalation Trajectory — Ongoing.** 
The Iran War was mentioned in 47% of ISM Manufacturing respondent comments in April
, making it the single largest driver of input cost uncertainty. Any material ceasefire or de-escalation would likely cause a rapid reversal of the energy inflation shock, collapsing the stagflation narrative. Conversely, Strait of Hormuz disruption or expansion of the conflict would accelerate the re-pricing of the Fed's neutral rate higher and trigger spread widening from historically tight levels.

3. **Fed Chair Transition (Kevin Warsh Confirmation Process) — 1-3 Month Horizon.** 
Kevin Warsh, the next Fed Chair nominee, previously supported reducing interest rates
, but he is also known as a market structuralist with hawkish instincts on inflation credibility. His confirmation hearings will be parsed intensely for any divergence from Powell's current "wait-and-see" stance. A dovish signal could reprice front-end rates down 25-50bps and provide a meaningful multiple expansion catalyst; a hawkish lean — particularly on fiscal dominance risks — could reverse the April ATH rally.

---

> **Bottom Line for the Portfolio Manager:** This is a regime that rewards **quality, pricing power, and earnings visibility** — not leverage, duration, or speculation. The S&P 500's new all-time high near 7,382 is fundamentally justified by record margins and a 27%+ Q1 blended earnings growth rate, but the 20.9× forward P/E leaves no margin for error on either the inflation or the geopolitical front. Stay long the compounders; hedge the energy shock via integrated oil exposure; and resist chasing small-cap cyclicals until the ISM employment sub-indices re-enter expansion territory.

## TIER 1 — OVERWEIGHT / BUY THE DIP

### 🥇 1. INDUSTRIALS (Defense / Electrification / Machinery / Aerospace)
**Relative Strength Tier: LEADER**


Caterpillar (CAT) and GE Vernova (GEV) have surged as they provide the heavy machinery and power systems required for the "Copper and Kilowatts" phase of the AI data center build-out.

- **⚡ Why Now:** 
The "physicality" of AI is taking precedence over the "virtual" — 2026 is about the copper, cables, and kilowatts, with companies like Caterpillar seeing renewed orders from the domestic manufacturing boom sparked by new fiscal policies.

### 🥈 2. FINANCIALS (Banks / Capital Markets / Insurance / Fintech)

 The combined CPI + PPI prints this week represent a material upside inflation surprise and reinforce a **stagflationary** tilt: inflation re-accelerating into the 3.8–6.0% range on headline metrics, while 
payroll employment in April came in at only **+115,000** — a softening labor market.
 The macro regime has shifted from "mildly restrictive hold" toward a genuine **higher-for-longer** posture with non-trivial hike risk on the horizon.

## 3. 🌩️ MACRO TAIL RISKS — Top 3 (12-Month Horizon)

### TAIL RISK #1 — **Stagflation / "Fed in a Box" Scenario**
**Probability Tier: ELEVATED (30%)**


Further policy discord out of Washington could exacerbate the current "stagflation lite" conditions. An increase in inflation to 3.5% or above would create conditions for a reversal in monetary policy, with the Fed quickly returning to a policy rate of 4% or above and the unemployment rate rising to 5% or higher. RSM attaches a 30% probability to this outcome.


**Trigger:** Tariff re-escalation (e.g., IEEPA-related court ruling reversal), oil shock from Iran conflict widening, or second-round wage inflation re-ignition. 
Surging oil prices since the Middle East conflict started on Feb. 28 have raised concerns that stubborn inflation could reignite.


**What gets hurt most:** Long-duration growth equities (PLTR, SNOW, NVDA, ADBE), TLT (massive duration blow-up), HY credit (spread widening), consumer discretionary (TSLA, SBUX, TGT). 
Stagflation would be most damaging for equities and credit spreads, but would also imply higher bond yields driven by breakevens and term premia.
 Winners: GLD, SCHD, energy (XOM, CVX, OXY, COP), short-duration Treasuries.

---

### TAIL RISK #2 — **AI Capex Disappointment / Hyperscaler Pullback**
**Probability Tier: MEDIUM (20–25%)**


With so much consumer spending and business investment reliant on AI-related stock prices and anticipated returns on AI, the economy remains vulnerable to any faltering of those two drivers. Just maintaining current spending levels would create a significant drag on GDP growth. A drop in AI-related spending next year could be enough to push the economy into a recession.


**Trigger:** One or two hyperscalers (Meta, Amazon, Google, Microsoft) signal CapEx tapering in H2 2026 earnings calls, citing ROI shortfalls. Or a credible competing AI architecture (open-weight models, custom silicon) disrupts the NVDA/AVGO oligopoly narrative. 
The key risks are that a fragile job market sparks recession fear or the equity market questions the value of AI-related revenues.


**What gets hurt most:** NVDA (-35%+), AVGO, MRVL, ALAB, AMD, ONTO — the entire AI silicon food chain. Also MSFT and GOOGL on valuation compression. 
If realised, equities would likely sell off while bonds would rally and credit spreads would widen sharply. If the recession is triggered by an AI-driven correction, it would likely also mean a substantially weaker dollar.


---

### TAIL RISK #3 — **Fed Independence / Fiscal Dominance Shock**
**Probability Tier: LOW-to-MEDIUM (15%)**


A key 2026 Fed development comes in May of this year when Powell's term expires. Trump has aggressively attacked Powell and the independence of the Fed, stating unequivocally that his nominee will need to enact his agenda of sharply lowering interest rates.


**Trigger:** A politically-appointed Fed chair signals premature rate cuts despite sticky inflation (3%+ CPI). Bond vigilantes respond by selling long Treasuries, pushing 10-year yields above 5.5%. Dollar weakens sharply, foreign capital flees U.S. assets. 
Any signs of more expansionary fiscal settings could add to potential pressures, as could renewed worries about Fed independence if the Administration again becomes frustrated about the pace of easing.


**What gets hurt most:** TLT (catastrophic), long-duration tech (SNOW, PLTR, ADBE, NOW), banks (BAC, regional exposure to duration losses), REITs (AMT, PLD, EQIX, O). 
Optionality on higher longer-dated yields should be protective against renewed worry about fiscal sustainability and Fed independence concerns. Upside exposure in gold and downside in the USD may help protect against institutional risks resurfacing.
